evidence_excerpt: At 14 years, renal function is normal (BUN 9 mg/dl; Cr 0.51), but hypokalaemia (3.1–3.4 mEq/l), metabolic alkalosis, impaired urine concentration, and bilateral nephrocalcinosis persist; no reported progression to renal failure.

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Persistent biochemical abnormalities and low urine osmolality (136 mOsm/kg) suggest ongoing issues, but no sustained symptomatic complaints or daily activity limitations are described.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: Thiazide diuretics were started based on NDI treatment, but discontinued 3 months later due to persistent failure to thrive.
At 18 months, indomethacin and spironolactone were initiated for hypokalaemia with high renin and aldosterone.
Indomethacin and spironolactone produced improvement in hypokalaemia and failure to thrive.
At around 12 years, bilateral nephrocalcinosis was identified by ultrasonography.
From age 12–14, hypokalaemia (3.1–3.4 mEq/l) and metabolic alkalosis persisted despite indomethacin and spironolactone.
Impaired renal concentration persisted (urinary osmolality 136 mOsm/kg) despite normal serum osmolality.

notes_on_uncertainty: The earliest documented diagnosis was impaired renal concentration at 1 year (with immediate treatment initiation). ERS (AI with nephrocalcinosis) was recognized later (~12 years). Event timing is approximated relative to the initial diagnosis; functional status and symptom burden are inferred due to limited explicit descriptions. No data on hospital readmissions or escalation beyond the reported medication changes.
